items.title: TLT bear flag target hit at 82 — reversal week in bonds
items.idea: TLT's bear flag had a measured target of 82; the weekly low was 82.70 — 'close enough for government work' — and the week ended with a reversal. Dale Pinkert says he 'hasn't seen this kind of price action in rates in a long time.' The 82 level is the market's litmus test for the new Warsh Fed's credibility. The 10-year touched 4.70% but no technical divergence yet to confirm a final top. The 2-year still doesn't look like rates have peaked, showing a steepening-to-flattening transition.
items.angle: If 82 TLT holds as support, it's the first real technical signal that rate pain is peaking. Watch whether bond shorts actually cover or this is just noise into a Memorial Day weekend with thin volume.
items.tweetA: TLT bear flag target was 82. Weekly low: 82.70. Then reversal week. Dale Pinkert: 'I haven't seen this kind of price action in rates in a long time.' Either this is the line in the sand for the Warsh Fed — or the next leg down is even uglier. $TLT
items.tweetB: 10-year touched 4.70% this week and the 2-year still doesn't look like rates have peaked. But TLT printed a reversal week at its bear flag target. Shorts covering into Memorial Day — or genuine rotation into duration? The next two weeks will tell. $TLT $IEF

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (35-min video session)

items.title: S&P and Nasdaq showing potential double tops — target 7,100 then 6,900
items.idea: Pinkert warns of double top formation across S&P and Nasdaq, with divergence on 4-hour timeframes. 'This high was confirmed. This time we're not going to confirm even if we make a new high.' He's looking for a pullback to 7,100 and, if that breaks, 6,900. He has 'even more conviction because of the divergence' this week. He doesn't say the bull market is over — just that a healthy correction is due regardless of whether a new high is made first.
items.angle: A 7,100 target on S&P implies a 5-7% drawdown from current levels. Not a crash call but significant if confirmed. The semiconductor non-confirmation is the primary tell.
items.tweetA: S&P 500 diverging on 4-hour timeframes. Even if it makes a new high, the internals don't confirm. Pinkert target: 7,100, then 6,900. 'Tops take time. Like turning an aircraft carrier.' I've been watching this divergence too. $SPY $QQQ
items.tweetB: Pinkert: 'Whether the S&Ps make a new high or not, I'm looking for a pullback to 7,100.' More conviction now because of divergence. If you want the canary — watch $SOXX leaders like Micron. They're already rolling over. $MU $SOXX

items.title: Semiconductors: three-drive formation, leaders not participating — distribution forming
items.idea: SOXX and SMH made new highs but Micron and SanDisk did not. Pinkert calls this a 'three drive formation' — internal participation is weakening even as the index makes new highs. 'Micron is a failing rally. SanDisk looks worse. Why aren't the leaders making new highs with the indexes?' If semis stall, he says bluntly: 'It'll take the whole market down.' Distribution is beginning at the top, not rotation into other sectors.
items.angle: Semiconductor non-confirmation at index highs is a classic late-cycle tell. $MU failing to lead while $SOXX prints a new high is the distribution setup that typically precedes market-wide selloffs, not sector rotation.
items.tweetA: SOXX made a new high. Micron didn't. SanDisk didn't. Pinkert: 'Why aren't the leaders making new highs with the indexes? These look like failing rallies.' Tops are built exactly this way — the index covers for the rot underneath. $MU $SOXX $SMH
items.tweetB: Three-drive formation in semis. Internal participation weakening at the exact moment the index looks strongest. This is the distribution setup. If semiconductors stall, 'it'll take the whole market down.' Not a rotation — a top forming. $SOXX $SMH $MU

items.title: Ags buy levels: Cocoa 6.18, Corn 450, Wheat 560-540 dream shakeout, Sugar 1,400
items.idea: Pinkert is constructive on agricultural commodities but wants specific retracement levels before entry. Cocoa: 'Nice pullback into a gap. This is retracement week. Put a price order in at 6.18 and risk the low.' Corn: '450 would be a good area to start looking.' Wheat: 'My dream scenario is we pull back to 560-540 in the coming weeks — just to shake out all the bullishness and all the people that got long food after the war started.' Sugar: looking for a break toward 1,400 to clear existing longs.
items.angle: The wheat framing is the most interesting: 'shake out everyone who got long because of the war.' The war trade is still long and wrong. The real agricultural trade requires the geopolitical longs to flush first.
items.tweetA: Ags levels from Dale Pinkert: Cocoa buy at 6.18 retracement. Corn: 450 is the entry. Wheat: 'dream scenario is 560-540 to shake out everyone who got long because of the war.' Sugar: waiting for 1,400. Patient, specific, tradeable. $WEAT $CORN
items.tweetB: Wheat target: pull back to 560-540 first to shake out the war-motivated longs. That's the move Pinkert is waiting for before getting long. The best ags trade isn't now — it's after everyone panics out. The war premium still hasn't been wrung out. $WEAT $CORN $SOYB

items.title: SpaceX S-1 hides the real story: 10 GW solar manufacturing facility at Bastrop, Texas
items.idea: SpaceX filed an S-1 talking about Mars, AI, and a $28.5 trillion TAM — but did not mention the 10 gigawatt solar manufacturing facility being built in Bastrop, Texas (1.1 million sq ft across two floors, 5 GW per floor). Bloomberg reports the purpose: powering next-gen Starlink satellites and orbital data centers. BEP Research: 'The disclosure layer is selling Mars and AI. The construction layer is buying power.' The Bastrop facility doubles in 2026 to add Starlink gateway antennas, solar cells, and AI compute satellites. The 10 GW scale is entirely absent from the filing.
items.angle: SpaceX is quietly becoming an energy/power company to fund orbital compute. At 10 GW of solar manufacturing capacity, this is larger than many national energy programs. The silence in the S-1 is deliberate — it would force the market to price it as infrastructure, not tech.
items.tweetA: SpaceX's S-1 talks about Mars. It talks about AI. It does NOT mention the 10 gigawatt solar manufacturing facility at Bastrop, Texas. 1.1 million sq ft. 5 GW per floor. Purpose: power for orbital data centers. The disclosure layer is selling the dream. The construction layer is buying power.
items.tweetB: Bastrop doubles in 2026 to add Starlink gateway antennas, solar cells, AND AI compute satellites. The S-1 names Bastrop but doesn't mention 10 GW. That omission is not an accident. The market is pricing orbital data center optionality that isn't in the prospectus.

items.title: $4T scarcity premium: OpenAI + Anthropic + SpaceX at 50x sales — the price of control, not growth
items.idea: OpenAI ($852B on $24B ARR = 35x), Anthropic ($900B+ on $43B ARR = 21x), SpaceX ($2T on $18B Starlink = >100x) sum to ~$4T on $80B combined revenue = ~50x. BEP's framework: 'The multiple is not tracking growth. It is tracking what each company controls that nobody else can easily replicate.' Notably, Anthropic is growing fastest (4.8x in 4 months) but pricing cheapest. SpaceX growing slowest on revenue but pricing richest. The inverse relationship between growth and multiple is the tell.
items.angle: If orbital data centers never scale to implied value, SPCX is the biggest public-market comp trap. But if scarcity pricing is real, every public AI infrastructure name (power, networking, foundry) deserves re-rating too. The public-market overflow from private scarcity is the actual trade.
items.tweetA: OpenAI: 35x sales. Anthropic: 21x sales. SpaceX: 100x+ sales. Anthropic grows 4x faster than SpaceX but prices at 1/5th the multiple. The multiple isn't tracking growth — it's tracking control. $4 trillion for three monopoly positions that can't be replicated. That's not a DCF. That's a ransom.
items.tweetB: Anthropic went from $9B to $43B ARR in 4 months. SpaceX's revenue is mostly flat vs. its Mars narrative. The market isn't confused about these valuations — it's deliberate. You don't price control points with earnings models. The question isn't if the multiple is right. It's whether you believe the moat is permanent.

items.title: Anthropic: 4.8x revenue in 4 months — $9B to $43B ARR, 1,000+ enterprise customers at $1M+
items.idea: Anthropic's annualized run rate was $9B at end of 2025. By April 2026 it reached $43B — 4.8x in four months. The Series G closed at $380B post-money in February 2026; a new round is in discussion at $900B+ pre-money on a $40-50B raise per TechCrunch. Over 1,000 customers at $1M+ ARR, with that cohort doubling in under two months. Enterprise revenue on track to reach parity with consumer by year-end.
items.angle: 4.8x in 4 months is not a normal SaaS growth trajectory — this has no historical precedent in enterprise software. The 1,000+ $1M enterprise customers doubling in two months means this is now a real enterprise revenue story, not API tinkering.
items.tweetA: Anthropic: $9B ARR at end of 2025. $43B ARR in April 2026. That's 4.8x in 4 months. 1,000+ customers at $1M+ ARR, doubling in under 2 months. A $900B valuation is being discussed for the next round. McDonald's does $25B/yr. Anthropic did that in one quarter. $AMZN $GOOG
items.tweetB: The fastest enterprise software ramp in history. Anthropic from $9B to $43B ARR in 4 months. The enterprise API migration is real and accelerating. If you're still modeling AI as 'hype' — what does 4.8x quarterly growth look like to you? I keep coming back to this number.

items.title: Anthropic profitable in Q2 2026 — two years ahead of schedule, $10.9B quarterly revenue
items.idea: Anthropic is set to turn a profit in Q2 2026, two years ahead of the company's own projections. Q2 2026 estimated gross revenue: $10.9B — more than the company's entire lifetime revenue to date — in a single quarter. Estimated operating profit: $559 million. Azhar: 'an extraordinary achievement set to feature in the annals of business history.' The profitability milestone changes the investment narrative for everyone betting against frontier AI valuations.
items.angle: Profitable AI lab at $40B+ annualized run rate shifts the debate from 'can they ever make money?' to 'how much can the model scale?' The $559M operating profit in a single quarter implies the cost curve is inflecting faster than expected.
items.tweetA: Anthropic will be profitable this quarter. Q2 2026 revenue: $10.9B — MORE than the company's entire lifetime revenue to date — in a single quarter. Operating profit: $559M. Two years ahead of schedule. The AI bust thesis just got a lot harder to sell. $AMZN $GOOG
items.tweetB: One quarter ago people were debating if LLMs could ever be profitable. Anthropic Q2 2026: $10.9B revenue, $559M operating profit. The inflection wasn't gradual — it was vertical. This is what product-market fit looks like at scale. $AMZN

items.title: AI backlash growing faster than AI revenues — Schmidt booed, AOC muddy water jar, physical infrastructure as target
items.idea: Eric Schmidt was 'solidly booed' at a college commencement. Another AI speaker was shocked by boos. AOC showed a jar of mud-brown water collected from a Georgia tap shortly after Meta started building a data center nearby. Azhar's framework: 'The narrative around AI has been about promises for tomorrow, but sacrifices today.' The physical manifestation — data centers, water use, construction — creates something tangible to protest. 'Fantasies or facts are fodder for the technorat. Globalization or NAFTA made a graph on some economists slide deck go up. Grid renewal and productivity excited the same neurons but don't speak to the heart.'
items.angle: The political liability of AI infrastructure is materializing faster than the economic benefits are distributing. This is exactly the pattern that preceded anti-globalization sentiment in the 2000s-2010s. Regulatory risk for US data center buildout is underpriced.
items.tweetA: Eric Schmidt: booed at commencement. AOC: showed a jar of brown water from a Georgia tap near a Meta data center. Azhar: 'Promises for tomorrow, sacrifices today.' The AI backlash is the same pattern as NAFTA opposition. A graph on some economists slide deck doesn't speak to the heart. Watch for bipartisan data center regulation. $META $NVDA
items.tweetB: The AI narrative problem: the people who will benefit from AI are not the same people living next to the data centers. That asymmetry created the trade war. It's creating this. College graduates being told AI will replace their jobs but trust the GDP graph — that's not going to land. The political backlash is early innings. $META $AMZN

items.title: Legal-mandate AI monopoly at 7x earnings: 35% revenue CAGR, 37.93% EBIT margin, debt-free
items.idea: HRMT profiles a small/micro-cap where demand is driven by legal mandates — customers are 'forced by law to pay every year' — and the company weaponizes AI to shrink processing costs and automate staffing, keeping operating margins locked at 18%. Three-year revenue CAGR: 35.40%. Three-year average EBIT margin: 37.93%. Trades at 7x trailing earnings on a debt-free balance sheet with a compounding cash hoard. 'Completely disguised as a sleepy legacy market anomaly' that institutional screeners miss due to dirty data.
items.angle: 7x earnings on a legal-mandate franchise with 35% revenue growth and AI-driven cost reduction is a remarkably unusual profile. The 'screeners miss it because of dirty data' claim describes exactly the kind of information asymmetry that produces outsized returns.
items.tweetA: Imagine a business where customers are legally required to pay every year. Add: AI cutting costs faster than competitors can react. 35% revenue CAGR. 37.93% EBIT margins. Trades at 7x earnings. Debt-free. This is the kind of small-cap that gets acquired before retail notices.
items.tweetB: The best compounder profile I've seen recently: legal mandate demand, AI used offensively to cut costs, 7x trailing earnings, zero debt, 35% revenue CAGR. The screeners miss it because the data is dirty. That's the whole point. Worth the research.

items.title: Only DJIA, SOX (barely), and QQQJ made new highs — SPX, Naz, IWM, QQQ all lower highs
items.idea: This week only the Dow Jones, a barely-higher SOX, and QQQJ (Junior/speculative tech ETF) made new highs. S&P 500, Nasdaq, IWM (small caps), and QQQ are all printing lower highs. Meisler notes this combination is unusual: 'Usually the DJIA and SOX don't move in tandem.' QQQJ outperforming QQQ signals speculative excess — the juniors beating the seniors is a breadth divergence that historically appears at market tops, not during healthy rallies.
items.angle: Lower highs across major indices with only speculative/junior tech leading is textbook late-cycle distribution. The QQQJ > QQQ relationship is the opposite of what you want to see when building constructive exposure.
items.tweetA: This week's breadth check: DJIA made a new high. SOX barely. QQQJ (speculative junior tech) made a new high. SPX: lower high. Naz: lower high. IWM: lower high. QQQ: lower high. The juniors outperforming the seniors isn't bullish. It's what speculation looks like at tops. $SPY $QQQ $QQQJ
items.tweetB: Usually DJIA and SOX don't move in tandem. This week they both made new highs while everything else — SPX, Naz, IWM, QQQ — printed lower highs. The breadth is deteriorating underneath a surface that looks fine. McClellan Summation still hasn't turned up. $SPY

charts.desc: Full breadth comparison showing new highs for DJIA/SOX/QQQJ vs lower highs in SPX/Naz/IWM/QQQ — visual divergence is stark. 20+ charts including McClellan, sentiment indicators, sector ETFs.

items.title: McClellan Summation refuses to turn up + Citi Euphoric + BofA Bull/Bear at 8.0 — maximum sentiment with breadth divergence
items.idea: Meisler calls the McClellan Summation's failure to turn up 'the biggest surprise to me.' The Summation measures breadth momentum and is not confirming new highs in leading indexes. Simultaneously: Citi Panic/Euphoria is at 'Euphoric,' and the BofA Bull/Bear Indicator is at 8.0 out of 10 ('Extremely Bullish'). Insiders are 'still asleep.' The triple signal — max sentiment + breadth divergence + insider apathy — is the classic setup for a hard reversal.
items.angle: Peak sentiment plus breadth divergence equals the classic near-term top setup. The BofA 8.0 reading historically marks near-term tops. The Summation not turning up while price makes highs is the internal tell that distribution is happening.
items.tweetA: Breadth divergence: McClellan Summation Index hasn't turned up. Citi Panic/Euphoria: Euphoric. BofA Bull/Bear: 8 out of 10 (Extremely Bullish). Insiders: still asleep. You don't need to be a bear to notice when everyone's already long. $SPY $QQQ
items.tweetB: The setup: BofA Bull/Bear at 8/10. Citi at Euphoric. McClellan Summation refusing to turn up. 10yr hysteria that everyone forgot by Friday. This is what the last mile of a rally looks like. Not calling the top — but not adding risk here. $VIX $SPY

charts.desc: McClellan Summation Index chart showing failure to turn up alongside price highs — this divergence chart is a must-save. Also: Citi Panic/Euphoria and Insider activity charts.

items.title: Warsh Fed = McChesney Martin redux: forward guidance heading to the 'dustbin of history'
items.idea: Ironsides draws the parallel between incoming Fed Chair Kevin Warsh and William McChesney Martin, who unwound WWII debt monetization. Warsh is expected to reduce interventionist policies and restore a 'neutral' central bank approach. First order of business: 'relegating forward guidance when the policy rate is not at the zero lower bound to the dustbin of history.' No robust debate expected about hiking at June meeting, but 'deep discussions around balance sheet policy, regulation, and forward guidance are probable.'
items.angle: Markets are priced for a Powell-like forward-guidance Fed. If Warsh executes a McChesney Martin playbook, volatility re-pricing of the forward curve is significant. The 'no more forward guidance' shift alone would add uncertainty premium to rates.
items.tweetA: Ironsides: Warsh is McChesney Martin, not Powell. Martin's era = no forward guidance, no zero-lower-bound coddling, neutral central bank. First Warsh agenda item: 'relegating forward guidance to the dustbin of history.' The rates market is not priced for this. $TLT $IEF
items.tweetB: Every market narrative assumes a Powell-style Fed that telegraphs every move. Warsh's model is the opposite. Martin era: take away the punch bowl when the party's going. If Warsh executes — the volatility adjustment alone is worth watching. $VIX $MOVE

items.title: Fed internal split: AI capex is supply-side, not demand inflation — hiking would only harm employment
items.idea: Ironsides highlights a growing tension within the Fed: some officials worried AI-driven capex boom could fuel inflation; others see it as productivity-enhancing and ultimately disinflationary. The key argument: 'Raising rates to counter AI-related investment would risk harming employment and broader economic growth without solving supply-driven inflation pressures.' This is a supply-side vs. demand-side inflation debate happening in real time inside the FOMC.
items.angle: If the disinflationary-AI view wins internally, it gives cover for Warsh to hold rates even as inflation stays elevated. If the inflationary-AI view wins, you get hikes into a productivity boom — potentially the worst policy error of the decade.
items.tweetA: Buried in the Ironsides note: the Fed is internally split on whether AI capex is inflationary or disinflationary. Hawks say capex boom fuels inflation, hike. Doves say it's supply-side, productivity-enhancing, hiking kills employment without fixing prices. This debate determines the rate path for the next 12 months. $TLT $SPY
items.tweetB: If AI capex is supply-side inflation — building capacity, not fueling demand — then raising rates to fight it is exactly the wrong policy. You'd be hiking into a productivity boom. The Fed has done this before. It doesn't end well. Which camp Warsh lands in matters more than any CPI print. $GS $JPM

items.title: The only hedge for the AI capex boom is cash — not non-tech sector rotation
items.idea: Ironsides' key recommendation: reduce risk, and the only effective hedge for the capex boom is cash — not rotating into non-tech sectors. In a Speculation regime with AI driving both the bull case and inflation uncertainty, non-tech sectors don't provide the same diversification they would in a normal late-cycle rotation. Higher energy costs are already squeezing corporate profit margins. Retailers (Target and Walmart) gave cautious commentary this week. Rotation into defensives doesn't solve the problem — it just swaps one form of risk for another.
items.angle: If the consensus 'rotate out of tech' trade is crowded AND non-tech is also struggling (energy costs, margin compression), then cash is the only truly uncorrelated option. Ironsides is making a contrarian call against the standard late-cycle rotation playbook.
items.tweetA: Ironsides' call: the only hedge for the AI capex boom is cash. Not XLP. Not XLE. Not XLV. Non-tech sectors don't provide the same protection in a Speculation regime where AI is simultaneously driving growth AND inflation uncertainty. Target and Walmart already flashing margin stress. Sit on cash.
items.tweetB: Everyone is rotating from tech into defensive sectors. But if higher energy costs are already squeezing non-tech margins — Target, Walmart this week — then defensive rotation doesn't hedge the risk. It swaps one problem for another. Cash is king. Ironsides is right.

items.title: Fed and Treasury actively suppressing MOVE index — unsustainable against persistent inflation
items.idea: Capital Wars argues the US Fed and Treasury are 'actively suppressing bond volatility (MOVE) to prop up liquidity and equities.' They frame this support as unsustainable 'given persistent inflation and tightening fundamentals, leaving markets at best range-bound with significant downside risks.' The MOVE index is effectively the bond market's VIX — suppressing it is a form of soft yield curve control, and the mechanism works until it breaks violently.
items.angle: If MOVE suppression is real and unsustainable, the snapback when it breaks will be violent. Bond volatility regime shifts from suppressed to normalized are historically what cause equity risk to reset — not gradual repricing. MOVE is the leading indicator to watch.
items.tweetA: Capital Wars: the Fed and Treasury are actively suppressing bond volatility (MOVE) to prop up liquidity and equities. But persistent inflation makes this unsustainable. When MOVE breaks out of its suppressed range — that's when equity risk gets repriced. Watch $MOVE. $TLT $SPY
items.tweetB: MOVE suppression as policy is the hidden risk nobody talks about. It's soft YCC — keeping bond vol down to prevent the equity feedback loop from triggering. It works until it doesn't. And when it breaks, it breaks fast. $VIX $MOVE $TLT

charts.desc: Capital Wars asset allocation schema showing Speculation regime — which quadrant we're in, and why it's the hardest.

items.title: Warsh faces the Burns/Volcker problem: takes helm in Speculation regime — hardest Fed entry historically
items.idea: Capital Wars maps all previous Fed chairs to their starting investment regimes. Warsh is starting in 'Speculation' — the hardest regime to manage because it 'usually means higher policy interest rates.' The only precedents who started in Speculation: Burns (1970-78) and Volcker (1979-87). By contrast, Powell, Bernanke, Yellen and Greenspan all started in more benign regimes. Burns failed to fight inflation and enabled stagflation. Volcker fought it but caused a deep recession.
items.angle: The Burns comparison is the more haunting one: a Fed chair who gets captured by the politics of the moment and enables inflation. Warsh's opening hand makes both the Burns outcome and the Volcker outcome possible — and the market is priced for neither.
items.tweetA: Every Fed chair who started in the Speculation regime either failed to fight inflation (Burns, 1970) or triggered a recession doing so (Volcker, 1979). Kevin Warsh starts there too. This is the hardest regime to manage. The market isn't priced for either of those outcomes.
items.tweetB: Powell, Bernanke, Yellen, Greenspan — all took office in benign regimes. Warsh starts in the Speculation quadrant: high volatility, late-cycle equities, flattening curve. The only historical precedents are Burns and Volcker. One inflated. One crushed. What does Warsh do?

charts.desc: Capital Wars regime map showing where every Fed chair started — visually powerful for the Burns/Volcker/Warsh parallel.

items.title: US debt >100% of GDP at 2x post-WW2 interest rates — 'double trouble' with no political solution
items.idea: Noah Smith explains the uniquely bad current debt situation: post-WW2 debt was >100% GDP but borrowed at 1.5-2% interest. Today's debt is similarly large (~100%+ GDP) but at much higher rates. The average maturity of US debt is ~6 years — so old cheap-rate debt is being rolled into expensive new rates. Smith: 'In double trouble. Our national debt is back above 100% of GDP — similar to what it was right after WW2. But now interest rates our government has to pay are almost twice as high as they were after WW2.' Neither party is politically positioned for austerity.
items.angle: The 6-year average maturity means the full rate shock from 2022-23 is still only partially transmitted to interest cost. The worst is literally still arithmetically ahead. This is not a forecast — it's an accounting identity.
items.tweetA: Post-WW2 debt was 100% of GDP at 1.5-2% interest. Today: 100%+ of GDP at 4%+ interest. Noah Smith: 'double trouble.' US debt average maturity is ~6 years, so the 2022-23 rate hike is still rolling through. The math gets worse from here — not better. $TLT $IEF
items.tweetB: Everyone compares current debt to WW2 and says 'we handled it before.' What they miss: WW2 debt was at 1.5% interest. We're rolling the same debt level at 4%+. That's not the same problem — it's 3x the interest burden. This is genuinely new territory with no clean historical playbook.

charts.desc: US net interest cost over time — the hockey stick since 2022 makes the structural break visually obvious. High impact chart for any debt discussion.

items.title: 100% of US deficit increase since pre-pandemic is from interest costs alone — not spending programs
items.idea: Smith shows that 'almost all of the increase in the budget deficit from before the pandemic is due to higher interest costs.' The structural deficit hasn't materially widened due to new spending programs — it's widened entirely due to interest on existing debt. Trump's spending is roughly equal to Biden's in GDP terms. What's changed is the rate paid on the same stock of debt. This reframes the entire political debate about what can be done about the deficit.
items.angle: Cutting spending by $100B doesn't solve a $400B interest cost increase. The debt problem is now primarily a rate problem, not a spending problem. Which means rate cuts are actually fiscal policy in reverse — and Warsh holding rates high is indirectly causing the deficit to grow.
items.tweetA: Almost all of the increase in the US budget deficit since before the pandemic is from higher interest costs. Not new programs. Not entitlements. Interest. Trump isn't spending more than Biden in GDP terms. But the rate on the same debt is 3x higher. Spending cuts alone can't fix this.
items.tweetB: The US deficit debate is targeting the wrong variable. Politicians argue about program spending. But 100% of the deficit increase since pre-pandemic is from interest costs. Cut every discretionary program to zero — you still have the same structural problem. The only solutions: grow, inflate, or refinance.

charts.desc: Chart inside: deficit decomposition showing interest vs. primary balance over time. Worth opening.

items.title: Debt spiral already underway — borrowing to pay interest, which increases interest, which requires more borrowing
items.idea: Smith states explicitly: 'The national debt continues to explode, because the government is borrowing money just to pay the interest on the money it borrowed before. This increased debt naturally results in even greater interest costs, forcing the government to borrow even more to fund those interest payments. And so on. Interest payments and debt just go to the moon.' He argues this is happening NOW, not a future scenario. Biden-era inflation eroded real debt value; that effect is now gone. Trump's first-term debt is rolling over at higher rates.
items.angle: A debt spiral in the world's reserve currency doesn't play out like Argentina — it plays out in long rates staying persistently elevated, gradual DXY erosion, and gold as the rational hedge. The 'it's different because we're America' argument weakens with every refund cycle.
items.tweetA: Noah Smith calls it explicitly: the US government is already in a debt spiral. Borrowing to pay interest → more debt → higher interest costs → more borrowing. 'Interest payments and debt just go to the moon.' This is arithmetic, not politics. $TLT $GLD $DXY
items.tweetB: The US debt spiral isn't a future risk. It's happening now. The 2022-23 rate hikes are hitting the interest bill. The interest bill now exceeds the primary deficit. The government can't grow or inflate its way out fast enough. Gold at $3,200 is not irrational. $GLD

charts.desc: Chart inside showing interest cost trajectory vs. primary deficit. Worth opening.

items.title: Venice AI: private AI inference on Base — cypherpunk case for the anti-surveillance inference layer
items.idea: Bankless makes a cypherpunk case for Venice, a private AI inference platform built on Base (Coinbase's L2). The premise: 'The cypherpunks knew that privacy had to be built, not granted. Venice is now carrying this torch for AI.' As AI inference centralizes around a few hyperscalers, every query to OpenAI or Claude is logged, stored, and used. Venice processes queries without logs, without retention, without surveillance — private inference as a crypto primitive on an Ethereum L2.
items.angle: If AI inference becomes as surveilled as social media, privacy-preserving inference layers have a large TAM. The cypherpunk-to-crypto pipeline (Tor for browsing → Bitcoin for money → Venice for AI queries) follows a consistent pattern of building privacy where permission won't create it.
items.tweetA: The cypherpunks built privacy because they knew it couldn't be granted. Venice AI is applying the same logic to LLM inference — private AI on Base, no logs, no retention, no hyperscaler surveillance. As AI centralizes, the need for private inference grows. Same as Tor for web. $BASE $ETH
items.tweetB: The next crypto narrative that makes sense: private AI inference. Every prompt you send to OpenAI or Claude is logged and stored. Venice (on Base) is building the alternative — private inference as a crypto primitive. Cypherpunk case is strong. $BASE $ETH

items.title: The Secret Life of Walter Mitty (2013) as peak globalization time capsule — 'a world that doesn't exist anymore'
items.idea: The author revisits The Secret Life of Walter Mitty (2013) and notes: 'It came out in 2013 which was such a different time in the world. When we were probably at peak globalization, the world was our oyster and the future seemed bright and endless. It's like a time capsule for a world that doesn't exist anymore.' The thesis: travel and action make daydreams reality. The subtext: 2013 was the zenith of the open world before deglobalization, AI anxiety, tariff wars, and geopolitical fragmentation reshaped the global landscape.
items.angle: The cultural marker of 2013 as 'peak globalization' is worth noting. A travel newsletter is implicitly doing better deglobalization analysis than most macro shops. The sentiment shift from 'world is our oyster' to 'a world that doesn't exist anymore' tracks perfectly with the secular investment shift.
items.tweetA: The Secret Life of Walter Mitty came out in 2013 — peak globalization. Ben Stiller skateboarding through Iceland, climbing the Himalayas. One author's take: 'It's like a time capsule for a world that doesn't exist anymore.' The macro shift is cultural before it's financial. We've been grieving peak globalization for a decade without naming it.
items.tweetB: 2013: the world was borderless, optimism was default, travel was transformative. 2026: walls, tariffs, AI anxiety. A travel newsletter just wrote the best brief on the deglobalization thesis I read this week. Not always in the places you look.
